title: Asynchronous Event-Driven Architecture (Azure Service Bus/Event Grid)
description: Event-driven architecture on Azure using Service Bus (reliable message queuing and topics with sessions) and Event Grid (event routing from Azure resources). Decouples producers from consumers via managed messaging; embraces eventual consistency with configurable dead-letter and retry policies.

cost.costNotes: Seeded via heuristics; validate against current pricing and expected workload (throughput, storage, retention, egress).

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: Azure Service Bus and Event Grid pricing varies significantly by throughput and features. Minimum ($50): Basic tier Service Bus with low message volume (~1M messages/month) or Event Grid with minimal custom topics (~100K operations). Maximum ($3000): Standard tier Service Bus with high throughput (100M+ messages/month), premium namespaces for critical workloads, multiple Event Grid domains with high event volume (1B+ operations), plus storage costs for dead-letter queues and message retention. Additional costs include: Service Bus messaging operations ($0.05 per million), Event Grid operations ($0.60 per million), storage for event schemas, and data transfer egress. Production workloads typically run $200-800/month for mid-scale applications.
provenance.adoptionCost: High-medium complexity pattern requiring significant architectural changes. Implementation involves: (1) Refactoring synchronous code to asynchronous event-driven model (40-80 hours), (2) Designing event schemas and contracts (16-24 hours), (3) Implementing publishers and subscribers with proper error handling, retry logic, and dead-letter queue management (40-60 hours), (4) Setting up monitoring, distributed tracing, and debugging infrastructure for async flows (24-32 hours), (5) Team training on eventual consistency patterns and debugging distributed systems (16-24 hours). Total: ~150-220 hours at blended rate of $50-75/hour with AI assistance = $3000-4000. Complexity factors include paradigm shift from request-response to fire-and-forget, handling message ordering and idempotency, and debugging non-linear execution flows.
provenance.licenseCost: Azure Service Bus and Event Grid are pay-as-you-go PaaS services with no upfront licensing fees. No commercial software licenses required - all costs are consumption-based and included in monthly runtime costs. Organizations may optionally purchase Azure support plans ($29-1000/month) but these are not mandatory for pattern adoption.

provides.reasoning: Event-driven architectures inherently provide temporal decoupling by allowing producers and consumers to operate independently without requiring both to be active simultaneously. Messages are persisted in the broker until consumed.
The asynchronous nature with message queuing provides natural buffering against traffic spikes, allowing consumers to process messages at their own pace without overwhelming downstream systems.

This pattern fundamentally delivers publish/subscribe capabilities, enabling one-to-many event distribution where publishers emit events without knowledge of subscribers, and multiple subscribers can independently consume the same events.

Event-driven architectures embrace eventual consistency by design, as events propagate asynchronously through the system and different components reach a consistent state over time rather than immediately.
Azure Service Bus/Event Grid/Event Hubs provide the streaming platform for this pattern.
Azure Event Grid and Event Hubs serve as event sources in this pattern.

requires.reasoning: Azure Service Bus and Event Grid are message brokers that are fundamental infrastructure requirements for this pattern. Without a broker, there's no mechanism to route events between publishers and subscribers.

Event-driven systems must handle duplicate message delivery scenarios (at-least-once delivery semantics). Consumers need idempotent processing logic to ensure correctness when messages are redelivered due to failures or retries.

Production-grade event-driven systems require dead-letter queues to handle poison messages and processing failures, ensuring messages that cannot be processed don't block the system and can be investigated separately.

Tracking message flow, identifying bottlenecks, and debugging failures in async systems is significantly more complex than synchronous patterns, making comprehensive observability critical for operational success.

supports_nfr.reason: Azure Service Bus Premium with zone redundancy can achieve up to 99.99% availability.
Event-driven architecture introduces message broker latency and async processing overhead.
Tail latencies include queue backpressure, retry logic, and message broker delays.
Async event-driven systems require time for queue recovery, consumer restart, and message replay.
Azure Service Bus Premium tier can handle up to 100k messages/second which equals 360M jobs/hour.
Event-driven architecture adds async overhead (100ms+ latency, message routing, consumer scaling). At very low throughput, simpler synchronous request-response patterns are more appropriate.
Pattern supports optional audit logging through Azure Monitor and diagnostic settings.
Event-driven architecture supports all tenant isolation models through topic filtering and routing.
Azure Service Bus supports GDPR compliance with data residency, encryption, and deletion capabilities.
Azure Service Bus is HIPAA compliant when configured with BAA and appropriate security controls.
Pattern supports CCPA compliance through Azure's data protection and privacy features.
Azure Service Bus supports SOX compliance with audit trails and immutable message logging.
Event-driven pub/sub architecture inherently provides eventual consistency without read-your-writes guarantees.
Pattern supports both strict durability with persistent queues and relaxed durability with in-memory options.

supports_constraints.reason: Pattern is Azure-specific, requiring Azure Service Bus and Azure Event Grid services.
Azure messaging SDKs support all major programming languages for event producers and consumers.
Event-driven architecture is a universal integration pattern supporting all platform types.
Pattern fundamentally requires async messaging as it is built on Azure Service Bus and Event Grid.
Event-driven systems can trigger AI inference workloads or operate without them.
Pattern supports both single-tenant and multi-tenant scenarios with topic-based routing.
Consumers can process events individually or batch them for efficiency.
Pattern supports both eventual consistency and distributed transaction patterns like sagas.
Azure Event Grid provides real-time event streaming while Service Bus supports traditional queuing.
Event consumers may include vector search capabilities but pattern does not require them.
Event consumers may persist to document stores but pattern does not dictate storage technology.
Event consumers may use key-value stores for state management but it is optional.
Event processing may involve graph databases but pattern does not require them.
Event data may be stored in time-series databases but pattern is storage-agnostic.
Event consumers may handle OLTP workloads but pattern supports all workload types.
Event streams can feed OLAP systems for analytics but pattern does not require them.

messaging_service.description: Azure messaging service: Service Bus for enterprise messaging with queues/topics, Event Grid for lightweight event routing with filtering, Event Hubs for high-throughput streaming scenarios

message_pattern.description: Messaging pattern: pub-sub for broadcast to multiple subscribers, queue for point-to-point delivery, competing-consumers for load distribution across workers

delivery_guarantee.description: Message delivery semantics: at-least-once ensures reliability but requires idempotency, at-most-once for best-effort delivery, exactly-once for Service Bus sessions with duplicate detection

dead_letter_enabled.description: Enable dead-letter queue for messages that fail processing after max delivery attempts, essential for debugging and handling poison messages

session_enabled.description: Enable sessions for ordered message processing and stateful workflows (Service Bus only), adds overhead but guarantees FIFO ordering per session

partitioning_enabled.description: Enable partitioning for higher throughput and availability across multiple message brokers, but prevents ordered delivery and sessions
